items.title: Consolidated Rules for 2026
items.text:
The Consolidated Rules for 2026 are the next unified expression of FedRAMP rules for cloud service providers, federal agencies, independent assessors, and advisors. The rules consolidate FedRAMP modernization work into a single public documentation set that explains how cloud services are certified, how agencies rely on those certifications, and how FedRAMP keeps the program aligned across government.

CR26 includes rules for FedRAMP 20x, Rev5, Certification Classes, Marketplace participation, Certification Data Sharing, Collaborative Continuous Monitoring, Vulnerability Detection and Response, Significant Change Notifications, and related program responsibilities.

The goal is to make FedRAMP rules easier to use in day-to-day work. Human-written pages explain context, roles, and practical workflows. Machine-generated pages will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules so that implementation details remain consistent with the source rule data.

items.title: FedRAMP Certification
items.text:
FedRAMP Certification is a government-wide security designation for cloud services that meet FedRAMP rules. A certification helps agencies understand whether a cloud service is appropriate for federal use and what responsibilities remain with the agency and provider.

FedRAMP Certifications are organized by type, class, and path. Certification types describe the broad authorization model. Certification Classes describe the risk and assurance level that applies to a cloud service. Certification paths describe how a provider gets to certification and how the certification is maintained over time.

Agencies remain responsible for authorizing their own federal information systems. FedRAMP Certification supplies reusable security information that agencies can use when making authorization decisions, managing ongoing risk, and monitoring provider performance.

items.title: Changelog
items.text:
This page will track material changes to the Consolidated Rules for 2026 documentation as the site is developed.

The initial CR26 site structure includes human-written narrative pages for orientation and machine-generated placeholders for rule pages that will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules. Future updates should identify changed rule sets, affected stakeholders, and the effective timing for each change.

items.title: Important Changes in CR26
items.text:
This page is retained for compatibility with the early site structure. The primary change log for CR26 is now the Changelog page, and effective-date details will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules.

CR26 introduces a consolidated rule structure for FedRAMP Certification, Certification Classes, FedRAMP 20x, Rev5 modernization, Certification Data Sharing, Collaborative Continuous Monitoring rules, Significant Change Notification rules, and Vulnerability Detection and Response rules.

items.title: Definitions
items.text:
Machine-generated content pending

This page will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules at a later date.

items.title: Shared Responsibility Model
items.text:
FedRAMP uses a shared responsibility model. FedRAMP, cloud service providers, federal agencies, independent assessors, and advisors each have responsibilities that support trustworthy use of cloud services across government.

FedRAMP defines government-wide rules, maintains the Marketplace, reviews certification information, and coordinates shared practices. Providers implement and operate cloud services, supply required security information, maintain evidence, and participate in ongoing monitoring. Agencies use FedRAMP information to make authorization decisions, manage agency-specific risk, and apply additional requirements when needed.

Independent assessors supply objective assessment and validation services. Advisors may help providers understand FedRAMP expectations and prepare materials, but they do not replace provider accountability or independent assessor judgment.

items.title: Source Data
items.text:
The Consolidated Rules for 2026 documentation is built from 2 primary source types.

Human-written narrative pages are drafted from FedRAMP modernization materials, program guidance, and stakeholder-specific context. These pages explain how the rules fit together and how agencies, providers, assessors, and advisors should use them.

Machine-generated rule pages will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules. Those pages should preserve the structure, labels, descriptions, purposes, applicability statements, and rule text from the source rule data.

When narrative content and machine-readable rules differ, the machine-readable rules should be reviewed as the source of truth for the generated rule content.

items.title: Critical Timelines for CR26
items.text:
Machine-generated content pending

Timing details will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules at a later date.

items.title: About CR26 Documentation
items.text:
The Consolidated Rules for 2026 documentation brings FedRAMP modernization materials together in a single site for agencies, cloud service providers, independent assessors, and advisors.

The documentation combines human-written orientation pages with machine-generated rule pages created from FedRAMP machine-readable rules.

items.title: Advisor Guidance
items.text:
Advisors help cloud service providers understand FedRAMP expectations, prepare materials, and navigate certification planning. Advisors can support readiness and coordination, but providers remain responsible for their cloud services and for the accuracy of information supplied to FedRAMP, agencies, and assessors.

Advisor participation should be transparent. Marketplace listing information and provider materials should make the advisor's role clear when advisor services are relevant to a provider's FedRAMP work.

items.title: FedRAMP's Role and Services
items.text:
FedRAMP defines rules for advisor Marketplace participation and may publish advisor listing information. FedRAMP does not endorse an advisor's work beyond the specific listing or recognition information supplied through FedRAMP channels.

Advisors should use FedRAMP rules to understand what information must be supplied for Marketplace listing and what claims may be made about advisor services.

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
Advisors should begin by understanding current FedRAMP Certification types, classes, paths, and Marketplace rules. Advisor services should be aligned with the current rules and should avoid outdated terminology or historical approaches that no longer reflect CR26.

Advisors seeking Marketplace listing should prepare clear service descriptions, contact information, relevant experience, and any required attestations or supporting information described by FedRAMP rules.

items.title: Marketplace Listing
items.text:
Machine-generated content pending

This page will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules at a later date.

items.title: Agency Guidance
items.text:
Federal agencies use FedRAMP Certification information to support authorization decisions, manage ongoing cloud risk, and coordinate with FedRAMP and other agencies. CR26 is intended to make those responsibilities clearer by separating government-wide FedRAMP rules from agency-specific authorization decisions.

Agencies should use FedRAMP materials to understand the provider's certification status, the certification class and path, the security information available through the Marketplace, and any additional agency requirements needed for the agency mission or system boundary.

items.title: Accessing Packages
items.text:
Agencies need access to security package information before making authorization decisions. Package access should support the agency's review of system boundaries, implementation information, assessment materials, plans of action, monitoring evidence, and other information needed for risk management.

When agencies request package access, they should be clear about the intended use and should protect package information according to applicable federal requirements.

items.title: Certification Classes
items.text:
Certification Classes organize FedRAMP Certifications by the level and kind of assurance required. Agencies should use the certification class to understand the expected security information, assessment depth, and monitoring obligations for a cloud service.

The certification class does not remove agency responsibility for authorization decisions. Agencies should still review whether the service, boundary, data types, and use case fit the agency mission and risk tolerance.

items.title: Cybersecurity Directives
items.text:
Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency emergency directives and binding operational directives may apply to federal agency use of cloud services. Agencies remain responsible for understanding those directives and applying them to their systems and contracts when applicable.

FedRAMP Certification information can support agency implementation, but it does not replace agency obligations under government-wide cybersecurity directives.

items.title: FedRAMP's Role and Services
items.text:
FedRAMP supplies government-wide rules, shared documentation, Marketplace information, and coordination services that help agencies reuse cloud security information. FedRAMP does not replace agency authorization authority or agency risk acceptance.

For agencies, FedRAMP's services include maintaining certification information, supporting shared security practices, clarifying common responsibilities, and helping agencies understand how to use provider and assessor materials.

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
Agencies should begin by identifying the cloud service, the intended federal use, the likely impact level, and the FedRAMP Certification information available for that service.

The next step is to review the Marketplace listing, package materials, certification class, certification path, and any monitoring information supplied by the provider. Agencies should then determine whether the FedRAMP information is sufficient for the agency authorization decision or whether agency-specific requirements are needed.

items.title: Government-wide Collaboration
items.text:
FedRAMP Certification is designed for reuse across government. Agencies benefit when common security information is supplied once, reviewed consistently, and kept current through shared monitoring and reporting.

Government-wide collaboration includes coordinated use of the Marketplace, shared expectations for provider evidence, common treatment of certification information, and consistent use of Collaborative Continuous Monitoring rules.

items.title: Legacy Materials
items.text:
Legacy FedRAMP materials may remain useful for historical context, but agencies should use CR26 rules and current FedRAMP modernization materials for current decisions.

When legacy language conflicts with CR26 terminology or current machine-readable rules, agencies should treat CR26 materials as the active reference point.

items.title: Marketplace
items.text:
The FedRAMP Marketplace helps agencies find cloud services, review certification status, and understand available security information. Agencies should use Marketplace information as the starting point for certification reuse and package access.

Marketplace information should be read together with provider-supplied materials, assessor-supplied materials when applicable, and any agency-specific authorization conditions.

items.title: Reporting Concerns
items.text:
Agencies should report concerns when certification information appears incomplete, inaccurate, outdated, or inconsistent with observed provider performance. Concerns may involve package information, monitoring information, Marketplace information, provider communications, or assessor-supplied materials.

Concerns should be specific, evidence-based, and directed through the appropriate FedRAMP or agency channel.

items.title: Agency Responsibilities
items.text:
Machine-generated content pending

This page will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules at a later date.

items.title: Sponsorship
items.text:
Agency sponsorship can help providers pursue a FedRAMP path that requires agency participation. Sponsorship should be based on an agency's mission need, intended use of the service, and ability to participate in the required authorization and monitoring activities.

Sponsoring agencies should coordinate with the provider, FedRAMP, and independent assessors when applicable so that responsibilities, evidence expectations, and timelines are understood before work begins.

items.title: 20x Overview
items.text:
FedRAMP 20x is a modernized certification path focused on automation, security outcomes, and reusable security information. Agencies using 20x materials should review the certification class, key security indicators, validation information, and provider-supplied evidence for the intended federal use.

20x is intended to make authorization decisions faster and more repeatable while preserving agency accountability for the systems agencies authorize.

items.title: 20x Authorization Tips
items.text:
Agencies reviewing 20x packages should focus on whether the provider's evidence satisfies the relevant 20x rules, whether the certification class fits the agency use case, and whether any agency-specific controls or conditions are required.

Agencies should document how the 20x package supports the authorization decision and where agency-specific risk decisions begin.

items.title: Certification
items.text: This page is retained for compatibility with the early site structure. Use the FedRAMP Certification page for current CR26 certification guidance.

items.title: Initial Authorization
items.text:
Initial authorization is the agency decision to authorize a federal information system that uses a cloud service. FedRAMP Certification information supports that decision by supplying reusable evidence, assessment results, provider commitments, and monitoring expectations.

Agencies should review FedRAMP materials, confirm the service is appropriate for the intended use, document agency-specific risk decisions, and identify any additional agency requirements that apply.

items.title: Additional Requirements
items.text:
Agencies may have requirements beyond FedRAMP's government-wide rules. These can come from mission needs, system architecture, data sensitivity, agency policy, law, contract terms, or other operational considerations.

Additional agency requirements should be stated clearly, tied to the agency's use of the service, and managed separately from the provider's government-wide FedRAMP Certification responsibilities.

items.title: High Controls
items.text:

items.title: Ongoing Authorization
items.text:
Ongoing authorization is the continuing agency determination that a cloud service remains acceptable for agency use. Agencies should use FedRAMP monitoring information, provider-supplied updates, vulnerability information, incident information, and agency-specific risk information to maintain that determination.

FedRAMP monitoring rules help agencies reuse common information while preserving agency authority over mission-specific risk decisions.

items.title: Agency-Specific Continuous Monitoring
items.text:
Agency-specific continuous monitoring covers the additional information an agency needs for its own authorization, mission, system boundary, or operating environment. These activities should complement FedRAMP's government-wide monitoring rules.

Agencies should avoid recreating government-wide monitoring requests when FedRAMP information already supplies what is needed. Agency-specific requests should be scoped to the agency's use case and risk decision.

items.title: Collaborative Continuous Monitoring
items.text:
Collaborative Continuous Monitoring rules support shared visibility into the ongoing security posture of FedRAMP-certified cloud services. Agencies should follow the Collaborative Continuous Monitoring rules when using shared monitoring information supplied through FedRAMP channels.

The goal is to reduce duplicative agency requests while improving consistency, timeliness, and reuse of provider-supplied monitoring information.

items.title: Rev5 Overview
items.text:
Rev5 remains an important FedRAMP certification path for cloud services that use National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53 control baselines. Agencies using Rev5 materials should focus on the provider's certification status, assessment materials, ongoing certification information, and any agency-specific authorization needs.

CR26 presents Rev5 guidance alongside FedRAMP 20x so agencies can understand which rules apply to each certification path.

items.title: Rev5 Authorization Tips
items.text:
Agencies reviewing Rev5 packages should confirm the service boundary, impact level, assessment scope, assessor findings, plans of action, and monitoring status before relying on the certification information.

Agency authorization records should clearly distinguish FedRAMP-supplied information from agency-specific risk acceptance and additional agency requirements.

items.title: Assessor Guidance
items.text:
Independent assessors supply objective assessment and validation services for FedRAMP. Their work helps FedRAMP, agencies, and providers understand whether required security outcomes have been demonstrated.

Assessors must maintain independence, follow applicable FedRAMP rules, and supply assessment information that is clear, complete, and usable by FedRAMP and agencies.

items.title: FedRAMP's Role and Services
items.text:
FedRAMP defines assessor recognition rules, performance expectations, Marketplace listing requirements, and rule sets for Rev5 and FedRAMP 20x assessment or validation activities.

FedRAMP uses assessor-supplied information to support certification decisions, monitor assessor performance, and improve consistency across the program.

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
Organizations seeking to provide independent assessment services should understand FedRAMP recognition rules, accreditation expectations, Marketplace listing rules, independence requirements, and the assessment or validation rules that apply to each certification path.

Assessors should prepare to supply objective evidence, document findings clearly, communicate issues promptly, and maintain the independence required for trusted assessment work.

items.title: Legacy Materials
items.text:
Legacy assessor materials may be useful for historical context, but assessors should use current CR26 rules when planning, performing, and documenting assessment or validation work.

When older assessor guidance conflicts with CR26 terminology or machine-readable rules, assessors should follow the current CR26 rules.

items.title: 20x Overview
items.text:
FedRAMP 20x assessor work emphasizes validation of security outcomes and reusable evidence. Assessors should understand the 20x certification class, validation expectations, and evidence rules before beginning work.

20x validation materials should be clear enough for FedRAMP and agencies to understand what was reviewed, how it was validated, and what risk remains.

items.title: Performance Standards

items.title: Initial Certification
items.text:
Initial validation under FedRAMP 20x focuses on validating the evidence and outcomes required for the provider's certification class. Assessors should supply validation results that are objective, traceable, and usable by FedRAMP and agencies.

The validation should make clear which evidence was reviewed, which outcomes were validated, and which findings require provider or agency attention.

items.title: Ongoing Certification
items.text:
Ongoing validation under FedRAMP 20x supports continued confidence after certification. Assessors may validate recurring evidence, changed implementations, or other rule-defined materials needed to maintain certification.

Ongoing validation should support reuse and should avoid unnecessary duplication of information already supplied under 20x rules.

items.title: Rev5 Overview
items.text:
Rev5 assessor work is based on National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53 control baselines and FedRAMP assessment expectations. Assessors help determine whether provider implementations satisfy the applicable requirements and whether assessment materials support agency and FedRAMP review.

CR26 separates human-readable guidance from machine-generated Rev5 rule pages so that detailed requirements can remain aligned with FedRAMP machine-readable rules.

items.title: Performance Standards
items.text:
Machine-generated content pending

This page will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules at a later date.

items.title: Initial Certification
items.text:
During a Rev5 initial certification, the assessor evaluates the cloud service against the applicable baseline, documents findings, validates evidence, and supplies assessment materials needed for FedRAMP and agency review.

Assessment work should be scoped to the certification class, service boundary, implemented controls, and FedRAMP rules that apply to the provider's chosen path.

items.title: Ongoing Certification
items.text:
During Rev5 ongoing certification, assessors support continued confidence in the cloud service by reviewing required monitoring evidence, validating selected controls or changes when required, and supplying clear findings.

Ongoing assessment should support Collaborative Continuous Monitoring rules and agency reuse of provider security information.

items.title: Certification
items.text: This page is retained for compatibility with the early site structure. Use the FedRAMP Certification page for current CR26 certification guidance.

items.title: Provider Guidance
items.text:
Cloud service providers use CR26 to understand how to prepare for FedRAMP Certification, supply required information, maintain certification, and work with agencies, assessors, advisors, and FedRAMP.

Providers remain responsible for their cloud services, the accuracy of supplied information, the completeness of evidence, and compliance with the rules that apply to their certification type, path, and class.

items.title: Additional Agency Requirements
items.text:
Providers may need to satisfy agency-specific requirements in addition to FedRAMP's government-wide rules. These requirements can reflect agency mission needs, data handling obligations, contract terms, system architecture, or agency policy.

Providers should distinguish additional agency requirements from FedRAMP Certification requirements and should coordinate with the agency on scope, evidence, timing, and acceptance criteria.

items.title: FedRAMP's Role and Services
items.text:
FedRAMP defines certification rules, reviews provider information, maintains Marketplace information, coordinates government-wide monitoring, and supplies shared guidance for provider participation.

FedRAMP's services help providers understand what to supply, how certification information will be reused, and how ongoing responsibilities are managed across government.

items.title: Legacy Materials
items.text:
Legacy provider materials may still be useful for historical context, but providers should use current CR26 rules for planning, certification, Marketplace listing, assessment, validation, and monitoring work.

When older materials conflict with CR26 terminology or machine-readable rules, providers should follow the current CR26 rules.

items.title: 20x Overview
items.text:
FedRAMP 20x is a modernized certification path focused on security outcomes, automation, and reusable evidence. Providers pursuing 20x should understand the required key security indicators, certification class expectations, validation approach, and ongoing information-sharing rules.

20x materials should be precise, current, and structured so FedRAMP and agencies can reuse them efficiently.

items.title: Initial Certification
items.text:
A 20x initial certification prepares the provider's evidence and validation materials for FedRAMP review. Providers should supply evidence that demonstrates the required outcomes for the chosen certification class and should make validation results easy to trace.

Providers should plan for automation where possible and should keep the evidence model consistent with current 20x rules.

The detailed requirement, control, materials, assessment, submission, and finalization pages will be generated from FedRAMP machine-readable rules.

items.title: Certification
items.text: This page is retained for compatibility with the early site structure. Use the FedRAMP Certification page for current CR26 certification guidance.

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
Providers should begin by identifying the service boundary, likely impact level, target certification type, certification path, and certification class. Providers should also determine whether they need an advisor, an independent assessor, agency sponsorship, or a Marketplace listing before pursuing certification.

Early planning should focus on evidence quality, automated security information where applicable, clear ownership, and readiness to supply information through FedRAMP channels.

items.title: Choosing a Certification Class
items.text:
Certification Classes describe the assurance and evidence expectations for a cloud service. Providers should choose the class that fits the service, data sensitivity, impact level, and expected federal use.

Choosing the wrong class can create rework. Providers should confirm the class with FedRAMP rules, agency needs, and assessor or advisor input when appropriate.

items.title: Choosing a Certification Path
items.text:
Providers should choose a certification path that matches the service's maturity, evidence model, agency demand, and intended certification class. Rev5 and FedRAMP 20x have different structures and expectations.

Providers should review path-specific rules before committing to schedules, assessor engagements, and Marketplace plans.

items.title: Choosing a Certification Type
items.text:
Providers should choose a certification type based on the service, intended federal use, agency needs, and the FedRAMP path that best fits the provider's security model and evidence capabilities.

The certification type should be selected before detailed planning so the provider can identify applicable rules, required evidence, assessment or validation expectations, and monitoring obligations.

items.title: Finding an Advisor
items.text:
Providers may use advisors to understand FedRAMP expectations, prepare materials, and plan certification work. Advisors can be useful when a provider is new to FedRAMP or needs help aligning internal security practices with CR26 rules.

Using an advisor does not transfer responsibility. Providers remain accountable for the accuracy of supplied information and for compliance with FedRAMP rules.

items.title: Finding an Assessor
items.text:
Providers that need independent assessment or validation should select an assessor that is recognized for the relevant FedRAMP work and is independent from the provider's implementation decisions.

Providers should coordinate scope, timing, evidence access, and expected deliverables before assessment or validation begins.

items.title: Implementing
items.text:
Providers should implement security controls, evidence generation, monitoring practices, vulnerability management, change management, and reporting practices before submitting materials for review.

Implementation work should be traceable to the applicable CR26 rules and should produce evidence that FedRAMP, agencies, and assessors can reuse.

items.title: Preparing and Getting Listed
items.text:
Providers should prepare clear service descriptions, boundary information, contact information, certification status information, and any required supporting materials before seeking Marketplace listing.

Marketplace information should be accurate, current, and consistent with the provider's certification path and class.

items.title: Rev5 Overview
items.text:
Rev5 certification work uses National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53 control baselines. Providers pursuing Rev5 should prepare control implementation information, assessment materials, plans of action, monitoring evidence, and package information that supports FedRAMP and agency review.

CR26 keeps Rev5 guidance available while aligning terminology and rule structure with the broader Consolidated Rules for 2026.

items.title: Initial Certification
items.text:
A Rev5 initial certification is the provider's first full assessment activity for a cloud service under the applicable Rev5 baseline and certification class. Providers should prepare the system boundary, control implementation information, required evidence, assessor access, and submission materials before the assessment begins.

The provider should supply complete, accurate materials and should respond to assessor findings in a way that supports FedRAMP and agency reuse.

items.title: Class A
items.text:
Class A is intended for a lighter assurance path where the applicable rules allow a reduced assessment structure. Providers should confirm that the service fits Class A before relying on this path.

Providers should review the machine-generated requirement, control, materials, assessment, submission, and finalization pages for the detailed rule text that applies to this class.

items.title: Class B
items.text:
Class B generally requires a stronger assessment structure than Class A and should be planned with assessor involvement, clear evidence expectations, and complete submission materials.

Providers should review the machine-generated requirement, control, materials, assessment, submission, and finalization pages for the detailed rule text that applies to this class.

items.title: Class C
items.text:
Class C applies where higher assurance expectations require deeper assessment information, stronger evidence, and careful coordination with FedRAMP and agencies.

Providers should review the machine-generated requirement, control, materials, assessment, submission, and finalization pages for the detailed rule text that applies to this class.

items.title: Class D
items.text:
Class D applies to the highest assurance expectations in this class model. Providers should plan for the most complete evidence, assessment, submission, and finalization activities.

Providers should review the machine-generated requirement, control, materials, assessment, submission, and finalization pages for the detailed rule text that applies to this class.
